reasoning: The unsafe findings are false positives: setup.py modifications are standard for Python packaging and executed safely within PKGBUILD context; core.py's third-party download is intentional functionality with legitimate source.
disagreement_analysis: qwen model misinterpreted standard Python packaging practices as unauthorized system modifications and flagged expected functionality as unverified download risk.
coverage_issues: Both models missed reviewing the .install script (woeusb-ng.install) which handles post-install actions
qwen model incorrectly reviewed unpatched setup.py version due to --noprepare execution
false_positives: qwen's setup.py verdict: Hardcoded paths are normal in Python post-install scripts and safely contained within PKGBUILD build process
qwen's core.py verdict: Rufus download is intentional feature with documented source; not a supply-chain risk
learnings: Prioritize review of PKGBUILD-controlled files (build/package functions, .install hooks) over source code
Apply patches before file review to avoid false positives from unpatched code
Clarify scope: Runtime software risks (e.g., core.py download) are out-of-scope for AUR build safety audits
Improve model training on Python packaging patterns to avoid misclassifying data_files operations as malicious
